name: using-git-worktrees
description: Use when starting feature work that needs isolation from current workspace or before executing implementation plans - creates isolated git worktrees with smart directory selection and safety verification
capabilities: Create isolated git worktrees with smart directory detection
Auto-detect project-local or global worktree locations
Verify .gitignore safety before creating project-local worktrees
Auto-run project setup commands (npm, cargo, poetry, go)
Run baseline tests to verify clean worktree state
Integrate with brainstorming and execution planning workflows
author: DYAI2025
securitySummary: This skill is documentation-only with zero executable code. All static findings are false positives: the hardcoded URL is legitimate metadata, hidden file references describe configuration paths, weak crypto patterns are false matches on directory names, and shell commands shown are example documentation for git operations. The skill contains no network operations, no credential access, and no actual command execution.
valueStatement: Managing multiple features in one repository is challenging when workspace switching disrupts your flow. This skill creates isolated git worktrees that let you work on multiple branches simultaneously with automatic safety checks and setup verification.
userTitle: Set up isolated git worktrees for parallel development
limitations: Requires git version 2.5 or later for worktree support
Does not manage worktree cleanup or deletion
Cannot create worktrees without an existing git repository

useCases.title: Parallel feature development
useCases.description: Work on multiple features simultaneously without switching branches or stashing changes

useCases.title: Isolated code review
useCases.description: Check out pull request branches in separate worktrees without disturbing your main development

useCases.title: Clean test environments
useCases.description: Create pristine test environments for each branch with verified baseline test runs

promptTemplates.title: Basic worktree setup
promptTemplates.prompt: Use the using-git-worktrees skill to create an isolated worktree for the feature branch called feature/authentication

promptTemplates.title: With project setup
promptTemplates.prompt: Set up a worktree at .worktrees/implementation with branch feature/refactor-api and run the full project setup

promptTemplates.title: With verification
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a worktree called worktrees/hotfix/critical-bug, run npm install, and verify tests pass before I start fixing the issue

promptTemplates.title: After brainstorming
promptTemplates.prompt: The design is approved. Use using-git-worktrees to create an isolated workspace for implementing the new feature we designed in brainstorming

bestPractices: Always verify .gitignore before creating project-local worktrees to prevent accidental commits
Run baseline tests immediately to establish a known-good state before making changes
Follow directory priority: existing directories > CLAUDE.md preferences > user preference
antiPatterns: Skipping .gitignore verification - leads to worktree contents being accidentally tracked
Proceeding with failing baseline tests - makes it impossible to distinguish new bugs from pre-existing issues
Hardcoding worktree directory locations instead of following project conventions

faq.question: What git version is required for worktrees?
faq.answer: Git version 2.5 or later is required. Check with git --version. Most modern git installations support worktrees.

faq.question: Can I use worktrees outside my project directory?
faq.answer: Yes. You can use ~/.config/superpowers/worktrees/<project-name>/ for global worktrees that live completely outside the project repository.

faq.question: How does this integrate with other skills?
faq.answer: The using-git-worktrees skill integrates with brainstorming, executing-plans, and subagent-driven-development for isolated implementation work.

faq.question: Is my data safe when using worktrees?
faq.answer: Worktrees are safe. This skill verifies .gitignore before creating project-local worktrees to prevent accidental commits of worktree contents.

faq.question: What happens if tests fail during baseline verification?
faq.answer: Report the failures to the user and ask whether to proceed with investigation or continue anyway. Never proceed silently with failing tests.

faq.question: How is this different from cloning the repository?
faq.answer: Worktrees share the same .git directory, so they use minimal disk space. Changes sync instantly between worktrees without pushing or pulling.
